sections.title: 不 (bù) — General Negation
sections.explanation: 不 (bù) is the most common negation word in Chinese. It negates present and future actions, habitual actions, and adjectives. Place 不 directly before the verb or adjective you want to negate. For habitual actions: '我不喝咖啡。' (I don't drink coffee.) For present states: '他不高兴。' (He is not happy.) For future plans: '我明天不去。' (I'm not going tomorrow.) Note: before a 4th-tone syllable, 不 changes to 2nd tone (bú). For example: 不是 is pronounced 'bú shì'.
sections.tip: 不 is for things that are generally or always true, ongoing states, and anything that hasn't happened yet. Think of it as 'not' for the present, future, and habitual.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I don't like coffee'?
exercises.explanation: Liking is an ongoing state/preference, so use 不 before the verb: 我不喜欢咖啡。(Wǒ bù xǐhuan kāfēi.)
Adjectives describing current states use 不: 她不高兴。(Tā bù gāoxìng.)

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'It's not hot tomorrow'?
exercises.explanation: Future states use 不, placed before the adjective: 明天不热。(Míngtiān bù rè.)

sections.title: 没 (méi) — Past Negation
sections.explanation: 没 (méi) negates completed actions and past experiences. It indicates that something did not happen or has not yet happened. 没 is short for 没有 (méiyǒu) — both forms are interchangeable when negating verbs. Key rule: when using 没, do NOT add 了 (le) to the verb, since 没 already cancels the completion. Correct: '我没吃饭。' (I didn't eat.) Incorrect: '我没吃了饭。' 没有 can also stand alone to mean 'don't have': '我没有钱。' (I don't have money.) The verb 有 (to have) is ALWAYS negated with 没, never with 不.
sections.tip: 没 is for things that didn't happen in the past. Never combine 没 with 了. And remember: 有 (to have) always pairs with 没, never 不.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I didn't buy it'?
exercises.explanation: Past negation uses 没 without 了: 我没买。(Wǒ méi mǎi.) Option C is wrong because 没 and 了 don't combine.

exercises.prompt: 我___有时间。(I don't have time.)
exercises.hint: 有 is always negated with this word
exercises.explanation: 有 (to have) is always negated with 没: 我没有时间。(Wǒ méiyǒu shíjiān.)

exercises.prompt: Which is correct for 'He hasn't arrived yet'?
exercises.explanation: 还没 means 'hasn't yet'. 他还没到。(Tā hái méi dào.) No 了 is needed with 没.

sections.title: 不 vs 没 — Key Differences
sections.explanation: 不 and 没 are not interchangeable. 不 negates the present, future, habits, willingness, and adjectives. 没 negates past completed actions and the verb 有. Compare: '我不看电视。' (I don't watch TV — habit.) vs '我没看电视。' (I didn't watch TV — past event.) '他不去。' (He won't go — future/willingness.) vs '他没去。' (He didn't go — past.) Common mistake: using 不 for past actions. '我昨天不去。' sounds unnatural — use '我昨天没去。' Another common mistake: using 没 with adjectives. '她没漂亮' is wrong — use '她不漂亮。'
sections.tip: Ask yourself: Am I talking about something that already happened? Use 没. Am I talking about a state, habit, or future? Use 不. For 有, always use 没.

examples.zh: 我不喝酒。/ 我昨天没喝酒。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ bù hē jiǔ. / Wǒ zuótiān méi hē jiǔ.
examples.en: I don't drink alcohol. / I didn't drink alcohol yesterday.

examples.pinyin: Tā bù qù. / Tā méi qù.
examples.en: He won't go. / He didn't go.

examples.zh: 她没有猫。(NOT: 她不有猫。)
examples.en: She doesn't have a cat. (有 → always 没)

exercises.prompt: Fill in: '我昨天___去上班。' (I didn't go to work yesterday.)
exercises.explanation: Past completed action (昨天 = yesterday) → use 没: 我昨天没去上班。

exercises.prompt: Which is correct for 'She is not tall'?
exercises.explanation: Adjectives use 不 for negation, not 没. 她不高。(Tā bù gāo.)

exercises.prompt: 'I don't have a car.' — which negation word?
exercises.explanation: 有 (to have) is always negated with 没有, never 不有. 我没有车。(Wǒ méiyǒu chē.)

sections.title: Special Cases
sections.explanation: Several common expressions use negation in fixed patterns. 不是 (bú shì) means 'is not' and negates identity/classification: '我不是学生。' (I am not a student.) 不要 (bú yào) means 'don't want to' or serves as a mild prohibition: '不要说话！' (Don't talk!) 别 (bié) is stronger and more colloquial for negative commands: '别走！' (Don't leave!) 别 is used only for imperatives, never for statements. Double negation creates emphasis or affirmation: '不能不去' means 'can't not go' = 'must go'. '他不是不知道' means 'It's not that he doesn't know' = 'He does know.'
sections.tip: 别 is only for telling someone NOT to do something (commands). 不要 works for both 'don't want' and commands. Double negation = strong affirmation.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Don't go!' as a command to a friend?
exercises.explanation: For imperative negation (commands), use 别: 别走！(Bié zǒu!) You could also say 不要走！

exercises.prompt: 我___是中国人。(I am not Chinese.)
exercises.explanation: 是 is negated with 不: 我不是中国人。(Wǒ bú shì Zhōngguó rén.)
不能不去 = can't not go = must go. Double negation in Chinese creates strong affirmation.
